label: 87_renminbi_rmb_reserve currency_internationalisation
description: The renminbi's growing role in global finance is discussed, highlighting its increasing use as an international currency and the challenges it faces compared to established reserve currencies like the US dollar and euro. The material covers recent developments and future prospects for the renminbi.

earliest_articles.title: A call to expand offshore market of renminbi
earliest_articles.excerpt: Obama calls for a stronger renminbi. Does he have real clout or is this just a publicity stunt to appease voters back home? China is the powerhouse of international finance (Chen et al 2009). It has accumulated the world's largest pool of currency reserves at $2.3 trillion. By imposing strict capital controls China…

earliest_articles.title: Is the Renminbi the Next Global Currency?
earliest_articles.excerpt: INTRODUCTION- The U.S. dollar’s run as the world’s stable currency has stumbled with the recent financial crisis. Waiting in the wings is the renminbi. But according to economist Geng Xiao, it’s still in China’s—and the world’s—best interest not to dump the dollar just yet. In this video interview, Geng Xiao, director…

newest_articles.title: Significant, but not systemic: The challenge of China's efforts to rival Western financial predominance
newest_articles.excerpt: Despite worldwide concerns about the US dollar, the Chinese renminbi is not yet ready to be a serious contender for leading international currency status. This Policy Brief examines three of the most important Chinese approaches to increasing the renminbi’s role as an international settlement currency: promote…

newest_articles.title: Internationalising like China
newest_articles.excerpt: With the third largest domestic bond market in the world behind the US and the EU, China is often described as a possible future international currency provider (Figure 1). central_articles.title: The renminbi’s unconventional route to reserve currency status
central_articles.excerpt: Why doesn’t the currency of the second largest economy in the world play a consequential reserve currency role? The share of China’s renminbi (RMB) in global reserve portfolios, at 3%, pales in comparison with those of the dollar (60%) and the euro (20%). What explains the difference? A common response is that the RMB…

central_articles.title: Will the Renminbi Rule?
central_articles.excerpt: The Chinese economy is now the world’s second largest and a key driver of global growth. It amounts to between 10 percent and 15 percent of world GDP (depending on how it is measured) and, in 2011, accounted for about one-quarter of world GDP growth.
